dialogue.utterance: Unit 17, 911 call for a suspected overdose in Richmond.
Hi ma'am, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's going on?
She was at the casino earlier. She's not responding now, her friend says she's a known heroin user.
Can you open your eyes for me? Her name is Jennifer, right, Jennifer? Do you know your birthday?
She told me her name is Jennifer. But I don't know her birthday.
Pushup test ready when you are, Jennifer.
No movement. Pupils are 2 mm bilaterally and non-reactive.
Airway is patent, no signs of obstruction. Let's maintain head tilt-chin lift to help.
Respirations are shallow and about four per minute. SpO2 is 90%.
Pulse is regular and around 90, BP 123 over 64.
Are you feeling any pressure or pain in your chest or elsewhere right now?
At this time, the skin assessment is being deferred due to the acute emergency.
The head and neck can be examined after we stabilize.
The mouth and throat are intact. No drainage or unusual odors noted.
Breath sounds are symmetric. Let's hold off on more detailed thoracic exams until after Narcan is given.
At this time, there is no indication for an abdomen exam.
No signs of urinary issues noted during this evaluation.
Peripheral pulses are present in all extremities. No edema or deformities observed.
Pulse is 90 and regular.
Respirations are at four per minute and shallow.
Oxygen saturation is 90% on ambient air.
Blood pressure is 123 over 64 mmHg.
Blood glucose is 0. The patient has not recently had food or drinks.
Jennifer, can you tell me if you have allergies or medications you're taking?
She doesn't have allergies and hasn't been on any medications that I know of.
What health conditions do you have that might be relevant, Jennifer?
She has mild asthma and a history of opioid abuse.
When was the last time you ate or drank something?
She might have had coffee this morning, nothing else since.
What were you doing before this overdose happened?
She was at the casino. Told me she smokes weed, but I think she did some heroin too.
Just to confirm, Jennifer, do you have Medicaid?
She said she has Medicaid, but her card is left somewhere.
The patient is not reporting any pain at this point.
I believe this is a suspected opioid overdose. Partner, let's prepare Narcan.
Starting 4 lead EKG monitoring now.
EKG is being run per protocol and the patient is showing baseline.
Narcan 1 mg administered intranasally.
Respirations improved to 12 after Narcan. Still shallow, but SpO2 rose to 100%.
Pulse is now 128, elevated and still regular.
Blood pressure is 166 over 72.
Blood glucose is up to 125. Looks like the response is starting.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia.
IV access established via extremity vein in left arm. Let's give second dose of 1 mg Narcan intravenously.
CV EKG monitoring is ongoing and consistent with protocol.
She's beginning to breathe more regularly. Resp improved to 16, clear breath sounds symmetric now.
She's beginning to move. Has eye opening, responds to verbal stimulus, pushing up.
Jennifer, do you know the date or time? Where are we?
I'm Jennifer. I am at a hospital?
Not yet, Jennifer. We're still in the ambulance, but we're heading to Chippenham Hospital. Let the nurse know if you feel unwell.
Pulse is 128, still elevated.
SpO2 remains at 100%.
Respirations are at 16, and patient is breathing comfortably.
Jennifer, are you okay with us transporting you to Chippenham Hospital for further care?
She is secured on the stretcher using all available restraints.
Most recent EKG is consistent with sinus tach, no new arrhythmias.
Jennifer is stable and continuing to be alert during transport.
ABCs are now intact, as the patient is breathing effectively and has spontaneous circulation.
She is fully AAOx4 now and shows no signs of pain or distress.
The patient has improved from altered mental status and now has intact airway, breathing, and circulation.
No evidence of trauma identified during assessment.
Pulse still at 128, maintaining through movement.
BP stable at 166/72.
Per protocol medical - altered mental status (protocol 3 - 15) we are following standing orders for naloxone.
Overdose per protocol 3-15 using standing orders, IV push administered to left antecubital.
Ma'am, we're handing you over to the ER. Let the nurse or doctor know if you need anything.
GCS improved to 15, no signs of respiratory distress or neurological deficits now.
Her known medical history includes: asthma, opioid dependence, and thyrotoxicosis. She received two doses of Narcan.
None of the prior vitals indicate hypotension or hypoxia. She is fully responsive and stable.
She is Jennifer and said she has Medicaid, but no card is with her.
Jennifer is stable, fully awake, and ready for further care at the ER.
Skin is warm, dry, and without rashes, abrasions, or bites.
No nuchal rigidity or neck masses noted. Pupils now reactive and 4 mm.
Ears and nose are clear, no drainage or obstruction.
No discharges or foreign bodies observed in the oropharynx.
Lungs are clear bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles heard.
Cardiac rhythm stable, no murmurs or tachycardia beyond sinus.
Abdomen is soft and not tender; no signs of pain or guarding.
Rectal bleeding and voiding habits are unknown at this point.
Patient has not voided since onset of symptoms; urine color is not assessed.
Visual and tactile exam of the back reveals no bruising or pain.
Patient is completely alert and denies any pain or discomfort now.
Jennifer, earlier you mentioned you smoke weed but someone also thought you might have done heroin. Is that correct?
I think I smoked some weed earlier, maybe that's it. I don't know.
I understand. People who have a history of opioid use can sometimes experience similar symptoms with other substances.
Rechecking SpO2 is 100% on ambient air still.
Respirations are now 12-14, regular and adequate.
Repeating EKG just completed and still shows only sinus tachycardia.
Jennifer is moving freely and conversing now. No restraints required for the remainder of transport.
Pulse still at 128, maintaining through movement.
BP stable at 166/72, cap refill not assessed at this time.
She will be loaded directly into the ER on a stretcher.
Transportation is proceeding under protocol 3-15 as per standing orders.
Transportation to Chippenham continues as per protocol.
She was unresponsive initially but has responded well to the intervention.
ABCs are intact, possibly due to improved oxygen saturation with pending IV.
IV was placed via extremity veins in the left arm during transport.
She now has full perioral color, intact PMS, no JVD observed.
Patient wished to thank us and appears cooperative for transport.
No further interventions needed at this time. All vital signs within safe limits.
